title: Copper mordant after-bath
subtitle: Using a dilute copper sulphate after-bath to shift and deepen plant dye colours on mordanted wool
excerpt: A copper after-bath is applied to already-dyed fibre to shift its colour rather than as a primary mordant. Yellows from plant dyes shift toward olive and khaki, greens deepen and cool, and oranges move toward brown when immersed briefly in a dilute copper sulphate solution. This technique extends a single dye bath into a range of related colours without additional dyeing.
sourceNotes: Copper as a colour modifier in natural dyeing is documented throughout dye literature and contemporary natural dyeing instruction. After-bath technique is observation-led and consistent with guild workshop practice.

attrs.body: Copper sulphate is toxic. Wear rubber gloves and use dedicated vessels not used for food.
attrs.heading: Safety

content.text: is a short second dip after the dyeing is finished. Copper sulphate here works as a
rather than a mordant. The dye is already locked to the fibre. The copper shifts the look by reacting with the dye at the surface.

items.name: Stainless steel or enamel dye pot (dedicated, not for food)

content.text: Fill the dye pot with enough warm water to cover the fibre generously (4-6 litres for 100 g). Dissolve the copper sulphate crystals in a cup of hot water, then add the solution to the pot. Stir well. The concentration should be 2-5 g copper sulphate per 100 g of dry fibre weight. A higher concentration produces a stronger colour shift but risks over-mordanting, which makes wool feel stiff.

Add the still-warm, damp dyed wool to the copper after-bath. The fibre should not be rinsed or cooled between the dye bath and the after-bath; transferring it damp and warm improves the modifier's uptake. Submerge the wool gently so all parts are covered.
Step 3: Heat gently and monitor the shift

Raise the temperature to 60-70 degrees Celsius and hold for 20-30 minutes, stirring occasionally. Lift a small section of the wool after 15 minutes to assess the colour shift: yellow will have moved toward khaki or olive, green will have deepened. Stop when the shift reaches the desired point. The colour will appear slightly paler when wet than when dry.

Remove the wool from the copper bath and rinse thoroughly in water of the same temperature. Rinse until the water runs clear. Dispose of the spent copper bath by taking it to a household chemical disposal point. Do not pour copper sulphate solution down the drain or onto garden soil, as it is toxic to aquatic organisms and soil invertebrates.

items.fix: Keep copper sulphate at 2-3 g per 100 g fibre for delicate wools and limit time in the bath to 20 minutes. Copper can degrade protein fibres at high concentrations.
items.cause: Concentration too high or time in the bath too long
items.problem: The wool feels stiff and harsh after the copper bath

items.fix: Not all plant dyes respond equally to copper. Flavonoid-based yellows (weld, onion, chamomile) shift well. Anthocyanin dyes (berries, red cabbage) do not shift reliably with copper.
items.cause: Concentration too low, or the original dye is not copper-responsive
items.problem: The colour shift is barely noticeable

items.fix: Keep the fibre fully submerged and stir gently every 3-4 minutes. Avoid agitating hard or the wool may felt.
items.cause: Fibre not fully submerged or stirred irregularly during the bath
items.problem: The colour is uneven with lighter and darker patches
